claim: Electrophysiological changes in motor neurons have been observed in patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.

result.claim: Electrophysiological changes in motor neurons have been observed in patients with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.
result.reasoning: The claim is strongly supported by multiple lines of evidence. Paper ID 17307849, "Intrinsic membrane hyperexcitability of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis patient-derived motor neurons," directly states that "hyperexcitability detected by clinical neurophysiological studies of ALS patients is recapitulated in induced pluripotent stem cell-derived motor neurons from ALS patients." This paper further details that motor neurons from ALS patients with SOD1, C9orf72, and fused-in-sarcoma mutations display hyperexcitability, and that SOD1(A4V/+) ALS patient-derived motor neurons have reduced delayed-rectifier potassium current amplitudes, which may underlie this hyperexcitability. Additionally, paper ID 37156706, "Reach task-associated excitatory overdrive of motor cortical neurons following infusion with ALS-CSF," reports that "Studies on human subjects with ALS reported the motor cortex to be hyperexcitable." This study also observed an increase in the firing rate of motor cortex neurons in rats infused with ALS-CSF, indicating electrophysiological changes. The consistency across these findings, from both human-derived cells and animal models, provides strong support for the claim.

evidence.text: Human neurons expressing apolipoprotein E4 (ApoE4) had higher levels of tau phosphorylation.
evidence.source: Gain of toxic Apolipoprotein E4 effects in Human iPSC-Derived Neurons Is Ameliorated by a Small-Molecule Structure Corrector

evidence.text: Induced pluripotent stem cell-derived motor neurons from ALS patients harboring fused-in-sarcoma mutations display hyperexcitability.
evidence.source: Intrinsic membrane hyperexcitability of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis patient-derived motor neurons.

evidence.text: Damage to ER-mitochondria signaling involving disruption of the VAPB-PTPIP51 tethers has been linked to the pathogenic process in Parkinson’s disease, fronto-temporal dementia (FTD) and related amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
evidence.source: The VAPB-PTPIP51 endoplasmic reticulum-mitochondria tethering proteins are present in neuronal synapses and regulate synaptic activity

evidence.text: SOD1(A4V/+) ALS patient-derived motor neurons have reduced delayed-rectifier potassium current amplitudes relative to control-derived motor neurons.
evidence.source: Intrinsic membrane hyperexcitability of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis patient-derived motor neurons.
